coredata.dc:title: Carbon-dots encapsulated luminescent metal–organic frameworks@surface molecularly imprinted polymer: A facile fluorescent probe for the determination of chloramphenicol
coredata.dc:creator: Pan, Mingfei
Sun, Jiaqing
Wang, Yixin
Yang, Jingying
Wang, Zhijuan
Li, Lin
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon dots (CDs)-encapsulated luminescent metal–organic frameworks@surface molecularly imprinted polymer (CDs@MOF@SMIP) was facilely prepared and applied as fluorescent probe for specific identification and sensitive detection of chloramphenicol (CAP) in food. Fluorescent CDs, serving as signal tags, were encapsulated within metal–organic backbones (ZIF-8), yielding luminescent MOF materials (CDs@ZIF-8). The synthesized CDs, CDs@ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP were investigated by morphological and structural characterizations (UV–Vis, XRD, FT-IR, BET, TEM). The CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP probe was demonstrated to have remarkable selectivity and sensitivity towards CAP. Its fluorescence decreased linearly with CAP concentration from 0.323 μg L-1 (0.001 μM) to 8075.0 μg L-1 (25.0 μM), featuring a low detection limit of 0.08 μg L-1. The CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP-based fluorescence strategy achieved satisfactory recoveries (95.5 % − 101.0 %) in CAP-spiked commercial foods with RSD < 4.4 % (n = 3). These results indicate that this method can effectively detect trace CAP in food matrices and has broad application prospects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs-encapsulated luminescent MOF
Surface MIP
Chloramphenicol
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP to CAP
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of CAP to CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP
Conditions optimization for the preparation of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP
Fluorescence response of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP to CAP
Kinetics evaluation of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP to CAP
Stability, reusability and selectivity of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Tianjin Municipal Science and Technology Program

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32272416 and 31972147 ) and Project of Tianjin Science and Technology Plan (No. 22ZYJDSS00030 ). The authors are grateful to Research Centre of Modern Analytical Technology ( TUST ) for their help in instrument support and data analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation process of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP.
TEM images of (A) CDs, (B) ZIF-8, (C) CDs@ZIF-8, (D) CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP; Elemental mapping image of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP (E - F).

(A) FT-IR spectra of ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP, and CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP; (B) TGA curves of CDs@ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP, and CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP; (C) XRD patterns of ZIF-8 (a), CDs@ZIF-8 (b), CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP (c), and CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP (d); (D) Nitrogen adsorption and desorption curves of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP and CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP.

(A) UV absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs, inset: the images of CDs dispersion under natural and UV light (365 nm); (B) EEM fluorescence spectra of CDs; (C) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8; (D) fluorescence spectra of CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP (a), CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP before (b) and after (c) combined with CAP; (E) UV–vis absorption spectra of CAP (a), CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP (b), and CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP combined with CAP (c); (F) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CAP (a) and fluorescence spectra of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP (b).

(A) Fluorescence responses of the CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP toward different concentrations of CAP; (B) Fluorescence responses of the CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP toward different concentrations of CAP; (C) Comparison of the Stern-Volmer plots for CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP with that for CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP (α < 0.05).

(A) Fluorescence stability of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP within 6 weeks; (B) The effect of irradiation time under natural light on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP; (C) Fluorescence responses of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP and SNIP to CAP and the competing antibiotics; (D) Fluorescence responses of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP in the mixed CAP and interfering antibiotics.

head.ce:title: Carbon-dots encapsulated luminescent metal–organic frameworks@surface molecularly imprinted polymer: A facile fluorescent probe for the determination of chloramphenicol

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety
Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health of Tianjin, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health of Tianjin
Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health of Tianjin, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, carbon dots (CDs)-encapsulated luminescent metal–organic frameworks@surface molecularly imprinted polymer (CDs@MOF@SMIP) was facilely prepared and applied as fluorescent probe for specific identification and sensitive detection of chloramphenicol (CAP) in food. Fluorescent CDs, serving as signal tags, were encapsulated within metal–organic backbones (ZIF-8), yielding luminescent MOF materials (CDs@ZIF-8). The synthesized CDs, CDs@ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP were investigated by morphological and structural characterizations (UV–Vis, XRD, FT-IR, BET, TEM). The CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP probe was demonstrated to have remarkable selectivity and sensitivity towards CAP. Its fluorescence decreased linearly with CAP concentration from 0.323 μg L-1 (0.001 μM) to 8075.0 μg L-1 (25.0 μM), featuring a low detection limit of 0.08 μg L-1. The CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP-based fluorescence strategy achieved satisfactory recoveries (95.5 % − 101.0 %) in CAP-spiked commercial foods with RSD < 4.4 % (n = 3). These results indicate that this method can effectively detect trace CAP in food matrices and has broad application prospects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Chloramphenicol (CAP) is the first broad-spectrum antibiotic entirely synthesized chemically on a large scale. Due to its excellent antibacterial activity against both Gram-negative and positive bacteria, CAP finds widespread use in treating various bacterial infections across human medicine, livestock and fishery breeding (Nguyen et al., 2022; Ma et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2020). However, many studies have affirmed that the abuse of CAP can lead to severe side effects, such as growth retardation, suppression of bone marrow hematopoietic function, causing granulocyte deficiency and even gray baby syndrome (Khoshbin et al., 2018). Long-term and sustained intake of food containing CAP residues can induce drug resistance and cause disruptions in the organism, posing a significant risk to human health. Many countries and organizations have clearly defined “zero tolerance” residue of CAP in food, meaning that CAP is not allowed to be detected in food products (Yan et al., 2018). However, due to the high antibacterial performance and low price of CAP, the illegal use or abuse of CAP still exists, seriously affecting the safety of food. Currently, the methods for detecting CAP residues are mainly based on instrumental analysis strategies such as chromatography-mass spectrometry, which have good sensitivity and reliability (Jia et al., 2021; Najafi et al., 2022; Nunes et al., 2022), but these methods usually require relatively cumbersome pretreatment processes and relatively expensive equipment. Other CAP detection strategies, such as electrochemical analysis (Banerjee et al., 2023; El-Moghazy et al., 2018), immunoassay (Wang et al., 2022; Guo et al., 2023), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (Xiao et al., 2020), have fast analysis speed and simple sample processing, but they have certain limitations in terms of sensitivity, stability, and accuracy. Therefore, it is necessary to develop an efficient, simple, stable and sensitive CAP assay.

In recent years, carbon-based nanomaterials have attracted a lot of attention from researchers for their cost-effective and environmentally friendly synthesis methods and excellent physical and chemical properties. Among them, carbon dots (CDs), the quasi-spherical particles less than 10 nm in diameter, stand out as a promising carbon-based nanomaterial (Li et al., 2021). The CDs not only combine the advantages of fluorescence tunability, high detection sensitivity, and resistance to photobleaching of organic molecular fluorescent probes and traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), but also have the features of green synthesis, low toxicity, simple and mild preparation process, and easy structural modification (Han et al., 2022). This lays the foundation for promising applications in bioimaging, drug delivery, and biomimetic sensing (Chen et al., 2023; Mohammadnia et al., 2022). Since CDs are prone to aggregation and fluorescence quenching, surface modification or integration into other hosts is essential for effective dispersion. The combination of CDs, known for their excellent optical properties, with a variety of functional materials can yield a range of nanomaterials with unique properties. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are organic–inorganic hybrid porous materials formed by the coordinated self-assembly of metals or metal clusters (nodes) with organic ligands (linkers). They possess distinctive characteristics such as large specific surface area, high porosity, good thermal stability, adjustable shape and pore size (Yang et al., 2019; Sakamoto, et al., 2022). Among the various MOF materials, the zeolite-imidazole framework material (ZIF-8) composed of Zn2+ ions and 2-methylimidazole stands out due to its simple synthesis, abundant pores, structural stability, and consistent performance (Maleki et al., 2020). ZIF-8 has found applications in diverse fields like catalysis, energy storage, sensing, and medical imaging (Hong, et al., 2023; Jiao et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2021; Nawaz, et al., 2023; Qu et al., 2021). ZIF-8, featuring a porous structure and large specific surface area, serves as a carrier for CDs, providing effective space that prevents CD aggregation. This method is effective in creating ideal luminescent MOFs (LMOFs) (Zhang et al., 2023). The LMOFs encapsulating fluorescent CDs, not only have good adsorption performance, but also can convert the response of the target being detected into the change in fluorescence intensity, which lays the foundation for efficient recognition and rapid detection.

Molecular imprinting technology (MIT) stands as a versatile and efficient tool for synthesizing synthetic receptors with specific molecular-recognition sites (Ostovan et al., 2022). The prepared molecularly imprinting polymers (MIPs) have drawn considerable attention in catalysis, drug delivery, and biosensing due to the merits of high specificity, good stability, ease of large-scale preparation, and excellent reusability (Li et al., 2021; Lusina & Ceglowski, 2022; Ding et al., 2020). Surface molecular imprinting technology (SMIT) refers to imprinting target molecules onto the solid-phase carriers like MOFs, silica, Fe3O4, etc. (Wang et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2022). This method evenly distributes imprinted recognition sites across the carrier surface. Unlike traditional imprinting, SMIT avoids the over-embedding and template removal challenges caused by high cross-linking, reducing the mass transfer distance between target and recognition sites and improving the adsorption separation efficiency of the target in practical applications. The synthetized MIPs can specifically identify and enrich targets in complex systems but require the combination with signal probes for the qualitative and quantitative analysis of trace targets (Bui et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2022). Therefore, the use of nanomaterials with fluorescent properties as the “core” carrier for surface molecular imprinting enables the imprinted recognition layer to obtain a specific fluorescent response to the target, facilitating the development of strategies that integrate specific recognition and highly sensitive detection. For example, the UCMPs@MIL-100@MIP sensor, employing the fluorescent UCMPs@MIL-100 as a carrier, can specifically identify and detect β-lactoglobulin in dairy products (Hong et al., 2022); the ratiometric fluorescent biomimetic sensor (Mg, N-CDs/Eu-MOFs@MIP) based on inner filter effect (IFE) and antenna effect (AE) synergy enables the fluorescence intelligent visualization analysis of oxytetracycline (Hu et al., 2022). The LMOFs have large surface area, high porosity, and stable fluorescence properties. As solid-phase carrier and signal probe to prepare LMOF@SMIP, they can effectively expand the surface imprinted layer and increase the number and uniformity of specific recognition sites, improving the adsorption performance and detection sensitivity. Therefore, LMOFs are considered ideal materials for developing fluorescent molecularly imprinting devices or strategies.

In this study, the synthesized CDs was encapsulated within the ZIF-8 framework structure to yield a LMOF material with remarkable fluorescence properties, which was further applied as solid-phase carriers to synthesize surface imprinted layer (CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP) that specially recognize the CAP antibiotics. The study aimed to establish a reliable, simple, stable, and sensitive approach for detecting CAP residue in food, offering valuable insights for advancing research on related materials and technologies.

The CAP (99.0 %) was bought from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China) and used as the target for detection. Structural analogs of CAP - thiamphenicol (TAP, 99.5 %) and florfenicol (FF, 98.0 %), and the broad-spectrum antibiotics amoxicillin (AMO, 99.0 %) and tetracycline (TC, 99.0 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) to test the specificity and anti-interference of the constructed fluorescent probe. Zinc acetate (Tianjin Fuqi Chemical Co., Ltd, China), 2-methylimidazole (2-MI), citric acid and o-phenylenediamine (Aladdin reagent company, Shanghai, China) were utilized in the synthesis of ZIF-8, CDs, and CDs@ZIF-8. Acrylamide (AM, TCI Development Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA, Aladdin reagent company, Shanghai, China), and 2, 2-azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN, Tokyo chemical industry Co., Ltd, Tokyo, Japan) were used for the synthesis of surface molecularly imprinted layers. The solvents ethanol and methanol were obtained from Sinopharm chemical regent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China), and ultra-pure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was supplied by the Milli Q purification system (Millipore, USA).

UV absorption spectra were analyzed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan). Infrared spectra were acquired with a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet iS50, Thermo, USA). The morphology of the prepared CDs, ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP materials was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos G2 200X, Thermo, USA) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Apreo, Thermo, USA). Thermal gravity analysis (TGA, Q50, TA, USA) was performed to verify the stability and composition of the fluorescent probe. Fluorescence spectra of the fluorescent materials were obtained using a fluorescence spectrometer (LUMINA, Thermo, USA) with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm.

The CDs were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method with a slight modification of the previously reported method (Xie et al., 2022). Briefly, accurately weighed citric acid (460.0 mg) and o-phenylenediamine (o-PD, 210.0 mg) were dissolved in 10.0 mL of ultrapure water. Subsequently, the mixed solution was transferred into a Teflon stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C (±2 °C) for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the synthesized solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and then dialyzed using a dialysis bag (1000 Da) in ultrapure water for 24 h to obtain the CDs suspension. Finally, the CDs solution was stored at 4 ℃ (±0.5 °C) in the dark for further use.

The detailed synthesis process of CDs@ZIF-8 was performed as follows. Zinc acetate (330.0 mg) and 2-methylimidazole (985.0 mg) were dissolved successively in a flask containing 45.0 mL anhydrous methanol. After ultrasonic mixing for 15 min, CDs suspension (5.0 mL) was added dropwise to the above mixture and left for 12 h. After the solution changed to a milky white colloidal dispersion, the product was collected by centrifugation, washed with ethanol three times to remove the residual reactants, and vacuum-dried at 60 °C (±2 °C) for 5 h to obtain CDs@ZIF-8 powder. Pure ZIF-8 was synthesized in the same process without the addition of CDs.

Fluorescent CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP was synthesized by modified precipitation polymerization process. The prepared CDs@ZIF-8 nanoparticles (30.0 mg) were dispersed into methanol (30.0 mL) in a round-bottom flask and sonicated for 15 min to form a uniform suspension. Subsequently, CAP (32.3 mg, 0.1 mmol) and AM (28.4 mg, 0.4 mmol) were added and magnetically stirred at room temperature for 3 h. After adding EGDMA (377.0 μL, 2.0 mmol) and AIBN (50.0 mg, 0.3 mmol), and deoxygenated by nitrogen for 10 min, the polymerization reaction was carried out at 60 ℃ (±2 °C) for 24 h. Upon completion of the polymerization, the template CAP was eluted with methanol until no UV signal of CAP was detected in the eluent. The preparation of CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP was carried out using the same procedure without the addition of CAP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP to CAP
ce:section.ce:para: Under the excitation wavelength of 370 nm, excitation/emission slit width of 20 nm and photomultiplier voltage of 500 V, the fluorescence response of 410–600 nm was recorded by LUMINA fluorescence spectrometer to study the fluorescence properties of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP and CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP materials. The prepared CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP or CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP material (2.0 mg) was mixed with methanol (2.0 mL) containing different concentrations of CAP in a 5.0 mL centrifugation tube. After shaking for 20 min in dark, the mixture was transferred into a standard 1 × 1 quartz cell to measure the fluorescence intensity.

This study utilized food samples in various state (milk, honey, and pork) which were susceptible to CAP contamination as actual samples to evaluate the practical applicability of the developed CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP fluorescent probe. The selected food samples purchased from a local supermarket (Tianjin, China) were determined to be free of CAP prior to use. Standard solution of CAP was added to the selected samples for the final concentrations of 0.5 μg L-1, 2.5 μg L-1, and 5.0 μg L-1. Milk (2.0 g), honey (2.0 g), and crushed pork (3.0 g) were accurately weighed and placed in a 25.0 mL plugged centrifuge tube, respectively. To the milk and honey samples, 4.0 mL of water and 4.0 mL of ethyl acetate were added, while the pork sample received 6.0 mL of ethyl acetate. After vortexing for 10 min, the mixture underwent centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 15 min. Then, 1.0 mL of the resulting supernatant underwent filtration through a 0.22 µm filter membrane and nitrogen blown to dry at 50 °C, and then redissolved in 1.0 mL of methanol. Following this, 1.0 mg of the prepared CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP probe was introduced into the sample solution (1.0 mL) and the mixture was shaken and measured the fluorescence intensity by the fluorescence spectrophotometer. The recorded change of fluorescence intensity was applied to calculate the amount of CAP in the sample.

The schematic diagram of the preparation and working process of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP probe was illustrated in Fig. 1 . The luminescent MOF (CDs@ZIF-8) was prepared by encapsulating fluorescent CDs into the MOF structure, and further served as a solid-phase carrier for surface molecular imprinting recognition system to construct a fluorescent sensor based on CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP for detecting trace amount of CAP in food. Encapsulation of fluorescent CDs within ZIF-8 structures facilitated effective CD dispersion, preventing its aggregation and fluorescence quenching, thereby enhancing the fluorescence stability of CDs@ZIF-8 composites. The surface molecularly imprinted layer introduced by precipitation polymerization improved the specific recognition ability and interference resistance of the sensing platform, ensuring a specific and stable fluorescence response to the target CAP. The constructed CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP fluorescence probe was demonstrated to provide an accurate and efficient approach for analyzing trace amounts of CAP in food.

The surface morphology and dimensions of the prepared CDs, ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP materials were observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the prepared CDs displayed a relatively uniform morphology with an average particle size of 3.6 ± 0.7 nm and good dispersibility. It could be clearly seen from Fig. 2B that the bare ZIF-8 had an average diameter of about 260 nm and showed a rhombic dodecahedron with a smooth surface. The morphology of CDs@ZIF-8, illustrated in Fig. 2C, remained largely unaltered compared to bare ZIF-8, indicating no significant change in the size after the addition of CDs. Conversely, the morphology of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP, as observed in Fig. 2D, changed from a rhombic dodecahedron (CDs@ZIF-8) to a rough spherical surface with a distinct core–shell structure, featuring an approximately 250 nm thick molecularly imprinted layer. The elemental mapping of the CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP composite (Fig. 2E, F) revealed a uniform distribution of Zn, C, H, O, and N elements, confirming the successful synthesis of the imprinted layer on the surface of CDs@ZIF-8.

The FT-IR spectra of CDs, ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP, and CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP were investigated in the study. As shown in Fig. S1, the characteristic peaks of CDs at 1710 cm−1 represented CO stretching vibrations, as well as 3423 cm−1 (OH and NH), 2924 cm−1 (CH), 1578 cm−1 (NH), 1385 cm−1 (CN and CH), and 1221 cm−1 (CO), indicating that the prepared CDs possessed –OH, –COOH, and –NH2 functional groups. For ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8, the characteristic peaks at 600–1500 cm−1 represented the vibration of the imidazole ring, and the peak at 3130 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CN bond in the imidazole ring (Fig. 3 A). In the spectrum of CDs@ZIF-8, the prominent characteristic peak at 1699 cm−1 was contributed to the CO stretching vibration, indicating the successful encapsulation of CDs into ZIF-8. The CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP and CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP showed characteristic peaks at 1628 cm−1 (–NH2), together with the peaks at 3606 and 3447 cm−1 (–OH), 2991 and 2954 cm -1 (CH), 1732 cm−1 (CO), 1455 cm−1 (CC), 1258 and 1158 cm−1 (COC), which were caused by the cross-linking of the functional monomer AM and the cross-linking agent EGDMA. The characteristic Zn-N peak associated with CDs@ZIF-8 were observed, demonstrating the successful preparation of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP probe. TGA analysis was performed in N2 environment to characterize the thermal stability and composition of the synthesized CDs@ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP and CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP materials. Fig. 3B depicted the change in material weight with increasing temperature. The weight loss of CDs@ZIF-8 from 450 °C to 750 °C was about 65.1 % (pink), which originated from the decomposition of the composites. The weight loss of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP (green) and CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP (blue) mainly occurred between 300 °C and 550 °C, with a drastic weight loss of about 92.8 % and 94.8 % for increasing temperature, which resulted from the decomposition of the imprinted layer and CDs@ZIF-8; and after 500 °C, there was no more obvious change in weight. It was noteworthy that the thermal weight loss of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP and CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP were much higher than that of CDs@ZIF-8, demonstrating the successful coating of the polymer layer on the CDs@ZIF-8 surface. Moreover, the curves of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP and CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP were consistent, both with only one weight loss process and almost no weight loss in the range of 30 °C − 300 °C, indicating that the synthesized CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP and CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP materials possessed remarkable stability.

Fig. 3C illustrated the XRD patterns utilized for evaluating the crystal structure of the prepared ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP, and CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP. The characteristic diffraction peaks observed in ZIF-8 closely resembled those reported in earlier studies (Ren et al., 2019). After encapsulating the CDs into ZIF-8, similar characteristic diffraction peaks with ZIF-8 were observed, confirming the retention of ZIF-8 crystal structure in the synthesized CDs@ZIF-8 product. Both the XRD patterns of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP and CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP exhibited resemblant patterns, featuring significantly reduced or even absent peak intensities in comparison to that of CDs@ZIF-8. This reduction in peaks validated the successful preparation of the fluorescent CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP probe, attributed to the encapsulation of the surface polymer layer. The porosity of the prepared ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP, and CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP were investigated through N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (Fig. 3D and Fig. S2). The specific surface area measurements revealed values of 1607 m2 g−1 for ZIF-8, 1560 m2 g−1 for CDs@ZIF-8, 13 m2 g−1 for CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP, and 11 m2 g−1 for CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP, respectively. The decrease in specific surface area from ZIF-8 to CDs@ZIF-8 indicated that the successful encapsulation of CDs within the ZIF-8 structure. Additionally, the subsequent reduction in specific surface area after wrapping indirectly demonstrated the formation of a polymeric layer (SMIP or SNIP) on the surface of the CDs@ZIF-8 material. The above results demonstrated the successful integration of CDs-encapsulated LMOFs with MIPs to prepare fluorescent sensing probes featuring core–shell structures.

The calibration curves of fluorescence intensity integration and UV absorbance of CDs and quinine sulfate (QS) were illustrated in Fig. S3A, and the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was calculated to be 49.8 % (Supporting Materials. 1 Calculation of fluorescence quantum yield of CDs). In the excitation wavelength range of 320–400 nm, the fluorescence intensity of CDs showed a trend of increasing and then decreasing, but the emission wavelength remained unchanged, indicating that the photoluminescence behavior of CDs had no excitation wavelength dependence (Fig. S3B). Fig. 4 A showed the UV and fluorescence spectra of CDs, with the maximum excitation wavelength corresponding to the UV absorption wavelength. The three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix (EEM) spectra revealed the CDs' emission wavelength at 465 nm and the optimal excitation wavelength at 370 nm (Fig. 4B). According to the changes in fluorescence intensity of CDs stored under different conditions, it could be concluded the inter-day RSD of 2.07 % for fluorescence stability (n = 6, 4 ℃ in dark), the decay rate of 6.61 % for photobleaching resistance (n = 6, 4 ℃ under natural light), and the inter-day RSD of 1.26 % for salt tolerance (n = 6) (Fig. S4). As shown in Fig. S5 A, the maximum fluorescence intensity of the composites was obtained during the synthesis of CDs@ZIF-8 with the addition of CDs at 5.0 mL. The emission wavelength of CDs@ZIF-8 was blue-shifted (445 nm) and its fluorescence intensity was slightly lower than that of CDs owing to the slight quenching of CDs fluorescence caused by the encapsulation into ZIF-8, tentatively demonstrating that the CDs@ZIF-8 material was successfully synthesized. The verification was further confirmed by the UV–Vis absorption spectra (Fig. 4C), where no distinct UV absorption peak of ZIF-8 was observed, while a noticeable absorption peak of CDs@ZIF-8 emerged around 368 nm, aligning with the characteristic peak of CDs. In addition, in order to investigate the distribution of CDs within the composites, the fluorescence intensity of the in-situ grown CDs@ZIF-8 and the self-assembled CDs + ZIF-8 materials before and after elution at 365 nm were compared (Fig. S5B). The CDs@ZIF-8 material emitted a distinct blue fluorescence, maintaining consistent fluorescence intensity before and after elution. In contrast, the CDs + ZIF-8 compound exhibited a dark green fluorescence, with no detectable fluorescence post-elution, confirming the encapsulation of CDs within the internal structure of ZIF-8. Fig. S6 illustrated the fluorescence intensity changes of CDs@ZIF-8 under different conditions, it could be concluded that the inter-day RSD of fluorescence stability was 0.87 % (n = 6, 4 ℃ in dark), and the decay rate of resistance to photobleaching was 12.62 % (n = 6, 4 ℃ under natural light), demonstrating that CDs@ZIF-8 had a stable optical performance. These results demonstrated the distinct structural features and superior fluorescence properties of the synthesized CDs, encapsulated within ZIF-8 with large surface area through in situ growth, providing stable fluorescence signals for the subsequent synthesis of a three-dimensional molecular imprinted system and laying the foundation for the rapid, sensitive and stable detection of CAP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of CAP to CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP-based fluorescent sensor for CAP detection was verified in this study. As shown in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP was quenched when combined with the target CAP, and after removing CAP, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP almost fully reverted to its initial level. This indicated that the prepared CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP material had a good fluorescence response to CAP. The mechanism of CAP-induced fluorescence quenching of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP was systematically investigated and verified. As observed in Fig. 4E, the UV absorption of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP after binding to CAP was only a superposition of the two with no generation of new chemicals, tentatively indicating a dynamic quenching. It was evidently seen that the UV absorption spectrum of CAP at 280 nm did not overlap with the emission peak of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP (Fig. 4F). Since the energy transfer required an overlap between the emission spectrum of fluorophore and the absorption spectrum of the target, the dynamic quenching of fluorescence was presumed to be due to charge transfer. Upon CAP interaction with the binding sites of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP, the charge from the conduction band of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP transitioned to the lowest unoccupied molecule orbital of the CAP. This process diminished the charge at the lowest vibrational energy level directly transitioning to the first excited singlet state, resulting in fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conditions optimization for the preparation of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the experimental parameters including the addition amount of CDs@ZIF-8 and the ratio of functional monomer AM to cross-linker EGDMA during the preparation of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP fluorescent probe were optimized in terms of fluorescence response (F 0 /F) and imprinting factor (IF). The synthesized CDs@ZIF-8 with stable fluorescence was used as solid-phase carriers which dosages will directly affect the detection accuracy and sensitivity of the CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP fluorescence sensor. As shown in Fig. S7A, different amounts of added CDs@ZIF-8 significantly impacted the fluorescence response of the prepared SMIP and SNIP to CAP. At 30.0 mg of CDs@ZIF-8, the values of F 0 /F and IF reached the maximum (1.5623 and 2.8134). Moreover, the ratio of functional monomer to cross-linking agent played a crucial role in the recognition properties of the prepared SMIP. The hydrogen bond formed between the functional monomer AM and CAP was the prerequisite for specific recognition. Appropriate amount of cross-linker can facilitate the curing of functional monomer, generating the cavities or binding sites matching the target, thus providing stable and well-defined spatial structures for three-dimensional surface molecularly imprinted systems. Excessive cross-linker will increase the thickness of the imprinting layer, resulting in excessive encapsulation of the fluorescence source (Zhang et al., 2023). This not only diminished the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP but also affect the mass-transfer rate and adsorption efficiency to CAP, subsequently affecting the sensitivity and detection speed. As shown in Fig. S7B, when the molar ratio of AM to EGDMA was 1:5, both F 0 /F and IF values reached the maximum (1.6181 and 3.0273). Therefore, these conditions were utilized in the subsequent experiments to construct a three-dimensional surface molecular imprinted system with stable fluorescence performance, high sensitivity and fast mass transfer efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP to CAP
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimized conditions mentioned above, the fluorescence response of the developed CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP probe to CAP at gradient concentrations (n = 9) was investigated. As shown in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence peak at 445 nm exhibited a gradual reduction with increasing CAP concentrations within the range of 0.323 μg L-1 (0.001 μM) − 8075.0 μg L-1 (25.0 μM), having a good linear relationship between the fluorescence response (F 0 /F) and CAP concentrations which was in accordance with the Stern-Volmer equation.
F 0
/ F
=
K SV
C CAP
+ 1

In which, F 0 and F referred to the fluorescence intensity of the CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP fluorescent sensor before and after adsorption of CAP, respectively. K SV was the quenching constant, C CAP was the concentration of CAP (μg L-1). According to the formula 3σ/slope (Sadler, 2016), the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.08 μg L-1. Fig. 5B demonstrated that at the same concentration of CAP, the F 0 /F response of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP was notably higher than that of CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP, indicating the formation of specific recognition sites for CAP by the surface molecularly imprinted layer. Comparing the slopes of the linear equations of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP (F 0 /F = 0.18559logC CAP + 1.10763, R 2 = 0.9992) and CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP (F 0 /F = 0.07362logC CAP + 1.03852, R 2 = 0.9849) (Fig. 5C), an IF of 2.5209 was calculated, providing further evidence of the superior specificity on CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP in identifying CAP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetics evaluation of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP to CAP
ce:section.ce:para: Surface molecularly imprinting significantly shortens the distance for mass transfer from the target to the specific recognition sites, effectively improving the detection efficiency. At the CAP concentration of 3.23 μg L-1 (1.0 μM), the adsorption kinetics of the prepared three-dimensional CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP were evaluated by monitoring the change of F 0 /F at different response times. As shown in Fig. S8, the fluorescence response of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP to CAP was superior to that of CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP for identical adsorption times. Initially, upon binding to CAP, the F 0 /F value of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP changed significantly, and the adsorption rate decreased after 10 min, but the F 0 /F value still increased slowly until reached the maximum and stabilized after 20 min, demonstrating that the adsorption-diffusion equilibrium was reached between CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP and CAP. The CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP could also reach adsorption saturation of CAP in a relatively short time. Since CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP lacked imprinting cavities and only possessed non-specific adsorption capacity, minimal variations on F 0 /F value were obtained. These results indicated that the CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP material formed specific binding sites corresponding to CAP. Moreover, the introduction of the CDs@ZIF-8 carrier enlarged the volume of the imprinting layer and increased the number of recognition sites, thus improving the adsorption capacity for the target CAP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, reusability and selectivity of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence stability of the CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP sensor served as a crucial characteristic parameter that laid the foundation for accurate detection of CAP. In this study, this feature was evaluated by comparing the fluorescence intensities of the CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP when stored under darkness for 6 weeks and continuously exposed to natural light for 12 h, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescent intensity of the developed CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP sensor remained nearly unchanged over a six-week period with RSD of 1.44 % (n = 6). Moreover, after continuous exposure to natural light for 12 h, the fluorescence intensity exhibited no significant change compared with the initial intensity with the fluorescence decay rate of 5.96 % (n = 6) (Fig. 6B). These results indicated that the developed CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP sensor possessed superior fluorescence stability and good resistance to photobleaching. The reproducibility of the CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP probe was further investigated by evaluating the changes in fluorescence intensity across five adsorption-elution cycles in methanol at CAP concentration of 3.23 μg L-1. When reaching the adsorption equilibrium, the captured CAP was eliminated, and the resultant particles underwent centrifugal collection for re-adsorption. Notably, although the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP declined after five cycles, it retained 94.02 % of its original value (Fig. S9). This might be attributed to various factors such as potential loss of the fluorescent carrier CDs@ZIF-8, incomplete removal of residual template molecules, or structural collapse of the fluorescence probe due to multiple washes. Nevertheless, the constructed fluorescence sensor exhibited consistent and reproducible response to CAP, demonstrating its potential for reuse in more than five cycles.

The SMIP layer on the CDs@ZIF-8 formed recognition cavities or sites that were complementary to the size, shape and functional groups of CAP, which was considered to have good specificity to CAP. The CAP structural analogs TAP and FF, and broad-spectrum antibiotics TC and AMO were selected to evaluate the selective specificity of the established fluorescence sensing system. The fluorescence response of CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP was compared with that of CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP for these five antibiotic targets. As shown in Fig. 6C, the CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP demonstrated the maximum F 0 /F (1.5982) value and IF (2.5527) specifically to CAP, higher than that of TAP, FF, TC, and AMO. In contrast, the CDs@ZIF-8@SNIP, lacking specific recognition sites, exhibited a relatively weak fluorescence responses (F 0 /F) with no obvious variations induced by the five selected antibiotics. The presence of specific recognition sites within the MIP layer effectively shielded the fluorescent sensor from interference by other antibiotics during CAP adsorption. Furthermore, the anti-interference ability of the prepared CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP was further investigated by adding equal amounts of interfering antibiotics into the CAP solution. As shown in Fig. 6D, even in the presence of interfering antibiotics, the constructed CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP probe displayed fluorescence response changes similar to that of the single CAP system, confirming its specific recognition of CAP and anti-interference capability. These results demonstrated its potential for detecting trace amounts of CAP in complex samples.

The practical capability of the prepared CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP fluorescent sensor in detecting CAP in animal-derived food samples was evaluated through the spiking and recovery experiments. Real samples milk, honey, and pork were determined to be free of CAP and spiked with different concentrations of CAP (0.5, 2.5, and 5.0 μg L-1 (or μg kg−1)). The fluorescence response F 0 /F of the CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP sensor was recorded for the calculation of the measured CAP concentration. The apparent recoveries of CAP were calculated according to the following equation, where C d and C s meant the detected and spiked concentration of CAP, respectively.
A p p a r e n t r e c o v e r y
%
=
C d
/
C s
× 100 %

As shown in Table S1, the apparent recoveries of CAP ranged from 95.5 % to 101.0 % across the three real samples at all spiked concentrations, with an RSD < 4.4 % (intra-day, n = 3), which were highly correlated with those of conventional ELISA, with a correlation coefficient of 0.99 (Fig. S10). These results indicated that the proposed CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP fluorescent sensing platform provided a reliable and stable strategy for the detection of CAP in animal-derived foods. Table S2 showed the comparison of the CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP-based fluorescent sensing method with other detection methods for CAP (Wu et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2019). The CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP fluorescence sensor constructed in this study was demonstrated to have significant advantages of wide linear range and low LOD, and more suitable for the specific identification and rapid detection of trace CAP in food matrices.

In this study, CDs with stable fluorescence performance, ZIF-8 with large specific surface area, and molecular imprinting technology with high specificity were combined to develop a CDs-encapsulated biomimetic sensor for CAP detection. This system experienced significant fluorescence quenching upon CAP detection, triggered by charge transfer. The integration of the surface MIP layer with the LMOFs enabled the CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP fluorescent sensor to have stable optical performance, remarkable sensitivity (LOD: 0.08 μg L−1), high selectivity and good reusability to CAP. The constructed fluorescent sensor yielded satisfactory results in rapidly detecting CAP in selected animal-derived foods. Therefore, the developed fluorescence sensing strategy based on CDs@ZIF-8@SMIP is advantageous and holds significant prospects for widespread application in food safety detection.

Mingfei Pan: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. Jiaqing Sun: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Yixin Wang: Validation, Methodology. Jingying Yang: Investigation, Data curation. Zhijuan Wang: Supervision, Formal analysis. Lin Li: Validation, Software. Shuo Wang: Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32272416 and 31972147) and Project of Tianjin Science and Technology Plan (No. 22ZYJDSS00030). The authors are grateful to Research Centre of Modern Analytical Technology (TUST) for their help in instrument support and data analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.138461.
